mission.title: Install and Configure Pipecd on Kubernetes
mission.description: PipeCD is a GitOps style continuous delivery platform that provides a consistent deployment and operations experience for any applications across multiple cloud environments. Installing PipeCD allows you to streamline your deployment processes and enhance your operational efficiency.

steps.title: Add the PipeCD Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the PipeCD Helm repository to your local Helm installation with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add pipecd https://charts.pipecd.dev
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install PipeCD using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install PipeCD in a new namespace called 'pipecd' with the following command:
```bash
helm install pipecd pipecd/pipecd --namespace pipecd --create-namespace --version v0.55.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the PipeCD pods are running successfully with:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace pipecd
```

steps.title: Port-forward the PipeCD API server
steps.description:
To access the PipeCD API server, run the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/pipecd-api --namespace pipecd 8080:80
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
You may want to configure resource limits for the PipeCD deployment. Edit the deployment with:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment pipecd --namespace pipecd
``` 
Add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of PipeCD will show all pods running in the 'pipecd' namespace. You can access the PipeCD API server at http://localhost:8080 after port-forwarding. Additionally, the resource limits should be configured as specified.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add pipecd https://charts.pipecd.dev
helm repo update
helm install pipecd pipecd/pipecd --namespace pipecd --create-namespace --version v0.55.0

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
